questions.text: Which of the following is a Zero Conditional sentence?

options.text: If it rains tomorrow, I will cancel the picnic.
If you heat water to 100°C, it boils.
If I were rich, I would buy a yacht.
If she had studied harder, she would have passed.

questions.explanation: Zero Conditional uses 'If + present simple, present simple' for facts that are always true. Boiling at 100°C is a scientific fact — it always happens. Options A, C, and D use different conditional types.

questions.text: The sentence 'If you press the power button, the computer turns on.' is a Zero Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + present simple (press), present simple (turns on)' — this describes an automatic cause-and-effect fact, which is the Zero Conditional structure.

questions.text: In Zero Conditional, which tenses are used in BOTH clauses?
questions.explanation: Zero Conditional always uses present simple in BOTH the if-clause AND the result clause. Example: 'If you mix red and blue, you get purple.' Both 'mix' and 'get' are present simple.

questions.text: Complete the Zero Conditional: 'If you __ water, it becomes ice.'
questions.explanation: 'Freeze' (present simple base form) is correct. Zero Conditional requires present simple in the if-clause. 'Will freeze' belongs to First Conditional; 'froze' and 'would freeze' belong to other types.

questions.text: The sentence 'If you don't water plants, they die.' is grammatically correct as a Zero Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + present simple negative (don't water), present simple (die)' — this is a valid Zero Conditional stating a general biological fact.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If you heats metal, it expands.'
questions.explanation: In Zero Conditional, the if-clause uses present simple. With 'you', use the base form 'heat', not 'heats' (which is for he/she/it). Correct: 'If you heat metal, it expands.'

questions.text: The sentence 'If ice gets warm, it melts.' is a Zero Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + present simple (gets warm), present simple (melts)' — melting is a physical fact that always happens when ice warms up. This is a perfect Zero Conditional.

questions.text: Which sentence is NOT a Zero Conditional?

options.text: If you add salt to water, it boils faster.
If you exercise regularly, you stay healthy.
If it rains tomorrow, I will bring an umbrella.
If dogs don't eat, they get hungry.

questions.explanation: Option C uses 'will bring', making it a First Conditional (real future). Zero Conditional states general truths using present simple in both clauses. Options A, B, and D all describe reliable facts.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If you will mix vinegar and baking soda, it fizzes.'
questions.explanation: Never use 'will' in the if-clause of a Zero Conditional. Use present simple: 'If you mix vinegar and baking soda, it fizzes.' This rule also applies to First Conditional.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If you touches a hot stove, it burns you.'
questions.explanation: With 'you', use the base form 'touch', not 'touches'. 'Touches' is for third-person singular (he/she/it). Correct: 'If you touch a hot stove, it burns you.'

questions.text: The sentence 'If the temperature drops below 0°C, water freezes.' uses Zero Conditional correctly.
questions.explanation: Correct! This is a perfect Zero Conditional: 'If + present simple (drops), present simple (freezes).' It states a scientific fact about the freezing point of water.

questions.text: Choose the correct Zero Conditional: 'If you __ enough sleep, you feel tired.'
questions.explanation: 'Don't get' is present simple negative — correct for Zero Conditional. 'Won't get' is First Conditional; 'didn't get' is past simple; 'wouldn't get' is Second Conditional.

questions.text: Which sentence is a correctly formed First Conditional?

options.text: If it will rain tomorrow, I stay at home.
If it rains tomorrow, I will stay at home.
If it rained tomorrow, I would stay at home.
If it had rained, I would have stayed at home.

questions.explanation: First Conditional uses 'If + present simple, will + base verb' for real future possibilities. Option A incorrectly uses 'will rain' in the if-clause. Option C is Second Conditional; Option D is Third Conditional.

questions.text: The First Conditional describes situations that are impossible or imaginary.
questions.explanation: Incorrect! First Conditional describes REAL, possible future situations. Example: 'If you study, you will pass.' (It's genuinely possible.) Second Conditional is for imaginary/unlikely situations.

questions.text: Which tenses form the First Conditional?

options.text: Past perfect + would have + past participle

questions.explanation: First Conditional = 'If + present simple, will + base verb'. Example: 'If she calls, I will answer.' The if-clause uses present simple; the main clause uses will + verb.

questions.text: The sentence 'If you will work hard, you will succeed.' is grammatically correct.
questions.explanation: Incorrect! NEVER use 'will' in the if-clause of First Conditional. The correct sentence is: 'If you work hard, you will succeed.' The if-clause must use present simple.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If she will call me, I will answer.'
questions.explanation: Remove 'will' from the if-clause. Use present simple instead: 'calls'. Correct sentence: 'If she calls me, I will answer.' This is a very common error to avoid in First Conditional.

questions.text: Choose the correct sentence:

options.text: If he will arrive late, we will start without him.
If he arrives late, we will start without him.
If he arrived late, we would start without him.
If he arrive late, we will start without him.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct: 'If + present simple (arrives), will + base verb (start).' Option A has 'will' in if-clause (wrong). Option C is Second Conditional. Option D uses base form without -s (wrong for he/she/it).

questions.text: Complete the First Conditional: 'If it __ tomorrow, we will cancel the match.'
questions.explanation: 'Rains' (present simple, 3rd person singular) is correct. In the if-clause of First Conditional, always use present simple. 'Will rain' is the most common mistake — avoid 'will' in the if-clause.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If they don't hurry, they will misses the bus.'
questions.explanation: After 'will', always use the BASE form of the verb (no -s, no -ing, no -ed). 'Misses' adds -s which is wrong. Correct: 'If they don't hurry, they will miss the bus.'

questions.text: The sentence 'If you eat too much sugar, you will get a toothache.' is a First Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + present simple (eat), will + base verb (get)' — this describes a real future consequence. First Conditional is perfect for cause-and-effect relationships about possible future events.

questions.text: Which sentence uses FIRST Conditional (not Zero Conditional)?

options.text: If you heat ice, it melts.
If bacteria grow in food, it goes off.
If you pass your exams, your parents will be proud.
If plants don't get water, they die.

questions.explanation: Option C is First Conditional: 'If + present simple, will + verb.' It's a specific future situation (you passing exams), not a general truth. Options A, B, and D are Zero Conditional — they describe facts that always happen.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If she study hard, she will pass the exam.'
questions.explanation: 'She' is third-person singular, so add -s/-es to the verb: 'studies'. Correct: 'If she studies hard, she will pass the exam.' Don't forget subject-verb agreement in First Conditional if-clauses.

questions.text: The sentence 'If you don't apologize, she won't forgive you.' is grammatically correct.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + present simple negative (don't apologize), will not + base verb (won't forgive)' — this is a valid First Conditional. Both positive and negative forms work in the same pattern.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If the team plays well, they will wins the trophy.'
questions.explanation: After 'will', use the base form without any endings. 'Wins' adds -s which is wrong. Correct: 'If the team plays well, they will win the trophy.'

questions.text: Choose the grammatically correct First Conditional sentence:

options.text: If we leave now, we will catch the train.
If we will leave now, we will catch the train.
If we left now, we would catch the train.
If we leave now, we catch the train.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct: 'If + present simple (leave), will + verb (catch).' Option B has 'will' in if-clause (wrong). Option C is Second Conditional. Option D uses present simple in both clauses (Zero Conditional pattern).

questions.text: Which sentence is a correctly formed Second Conditional?

options.text: If I am rich, I will buy a mansion.
If I were rich, I would buy a mansion.
If I am rich, I would buy a mansion.
If I will be rich, I would buy a mansion.

questions.explanation: Second Conditional uses 'If + past simple, would + base verb' for imaginary or unlikely situations. Note: use 'were' (not 'was') for all persons in formal Second Conditional. Option A is First Conditional; C and D mix tenses incorrectly.

questions.text: Second Conditional describes situations that are real and likely to happen in the future.
questions.explanation: Incorrect! Second Conditional describes IMAGINARY or UNLIKELY situations. Example: 'If I were a bird, I would fly.' (You're not a bird — it's imaginary.) First Conditional is for real, possible futures.

questions.text: Which tenses form the Second Conditional?

options.text: Past perfect + would have + past participle

questions.explanation: Second Conditional = 'If + past simple, would + base verb'. Example: 'If she knew the answer, she would tell us.' The past simple in the if-clause signals unreality, NOT past time.

questions.text: In Second Conditional, it is more formal and grammatically preferred to use 'were' instead of 'was' for all subjects, including 'I'.
questions.explanation: Correct! In Second Conditional, 'were' is preferred for all subjects: 'If I were you...', 'If he were here...'. While 'was' is common in informal speech, 'were' is the standard in written and formal English.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If I was you, I would apologize immediately.'
questions.explanation: In Second Conditional, use 'were' for all subjects (not 'was'). Correct: 'If I were you, I would apologize immediately.' The expression 'If I were you' is a fixed phrase meaning 'in your situation'.

questions.text: Complete the Second Conditional: 'If dogs could talk, they __ ask for more walks.'
questions.explanation: 'Would' is correct for Second Conditional. Dogs cannot really talk — this is imaginary. 'Will' belongs to First Conditional (real future). 'Would ask' signals that the situation is hypothetical.

questions.text: What is wrong with: 'If she knew the answer, she will tell us.'?
questions.explanation: The if-clause 'If she knew' uses past simple, signalling Second Conditional. The main clause must use 'would', not 'will'. 'Will' is for First Conditional (real future). Correct: 'If she knew the answer, she would tell us.'

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If he had more time, he will learn the guitar.'
questions.explanation: 'Had' in the if-clause shows this is Second Conditional (imaginary present). The main clause must use 'would', not 'will'. Correct: 'If he had more time, he would learn the guitar.'

questions.text: The sentence 'If I were taller, I would join the basketball team.' is grammatically correct.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + past simple (were), would + base verb (join)' — perfect Second Conditional. Being tall enough for basketball is imagined (the speaker is not tall enough now), which is why Second Conditional is used.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If she lived closer to school, she will walk every day.'
questions.explanation: The if-clause 'If she lived closer' uses past simple, indicating Second Conditional (she doesn't live close — imaginary). The main clause must use 'would walk', not 'will walk'. 'Will' is only for First Conditional.

questions.text: Which sentence is Second Conditional (imaginary), NOT First Conditional (real)?

options.text: If it snows tomorrow, school will be cancelled.
If you study, you will improve.
If I had wings, I would fly to school.
If she calls, I will answer.

questions.explanation: Option C is Second Conditional — having wings is imaginary/impossible. Options A, B, and D are all First Conditional because they describe genuinely possible future events.

questions.text: The sentence 'If I were the teacher, I would give less homework.' is a Second Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! The speaker is not the teacher (imaginary situation). 'If + past simple (were), would + verb (give)' — this is a perfect Second Conditional expressing a hypothetical wish.

questions.text: Which sentence is a correctly formed Third Conditional?

options.text: If I study hard, I will pass.
If I studied hard, I would pass.
If I had studied hard, I would have passed.
If I have studied hard, I will have passed.

questions.explanation: Third Conditional uses 'If + past perfect (had + past participle), would have + past participle' for imaginary changes to past situations. Option A is First; Option B is Second; Option D mixes tenses incorrectly.

questions.text: Third Conditional is used to talk about situations that actually happened in the past.
questions.explanation: Incorrect! Third Conditional talks about IMAGINARY changes to the past — things that did NOT happen. Example: 'If she had studied, she would have passed.' (She didn't study and didn't pass — we're imagining a different past.)

questions.text: In Third Conditional, what form does the if-clause use?
questions.explanation: Third Conditional if-clause uses PAST PERFECT: 'had + past participle'. Examples: 'If she had called...', 'If they had arrived...', 'If I had known...'. This past perfect signals we're imagining a different past.

questions.text: The sentence 'If he had left earlier, he would have caught the train.' is grammatically correct Third Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + past perfect (had left), would have + past participle (would have caught)' — perfect Third Conditional. The speaker implies he did NOT leave early and did NOT catch the train.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If they had brought a map, they would lose their way.'
questions.explanation: In Third Conditional, the main clause uses 'would have + past participle'. 'Would lose' is Second Conditional. Also, the meaning should be negative (they wouldn't have gotten lost). Correct: 'If they had brought a map, they would not have lost their way.'

questions.text: Complete the Third Conditional: 'If she __ earlier, she would have caught the flight.'
questions.explanation: 'Had left' (past perfect) is correct for the if-clause of Third Conditional. 'Left' alone is past simple (Second Conditional). 'Would leave' cannot go in the if-clause. 'Has left' is present perfect (wrong).

questions.text: What is wrong with: 'If we had booked earlier, we would save money.'?
questions.explanation: The if-clause 'If we had booked' uses past perfect — Third Conditional. The main clause must use 'would have + past participle': 'would have saved'. Using 'would save' (without 'have') is a very common mistake.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If I had known about the sale, I will have bought more.'
questions.explanation: The main clause of Third Conditional uses 'WOULD have + past participle', not 'will have'. 'Will' cannot be used in Third Conditional. Correct: 'If I had known about the sale, I would have bought more.'

questions.text: The sentence 'If the team had practised more, they wouldn't have lost the final.' is Third Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + past perfect (had practised), would not have + past participle (wouldn't have lost)' — perfect Third Conditional. The team did not practise enough, and they did lose the final. We're imagining a different outcome.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If she had taken the medicine, she would recovered faster.'
questions.explanation: Third Conditional main clause requires 'would HAVE + past participle'. 'Would recovered' is missing 'have'. Correct: 'If she had taken the medicine, she would have recovered faster.' Never omit 'have' in Third Conditional.

questions.text: Which correctly completes this Third Conditional? 'If he had studied the map, he __ lost.'
questions.explanation: 'Wouldn't have been' is correct: 'would not have + past participle (been)'. Option A ('wouldn't be') is Second Conditional. Option B uses 'won't' (wrong — needs 'would'). Option D has wrong form 'had been' after 'wouldn't'.

questions.text: The sentence 'If they had listened to the teacher, they would have understood the lesson.' uses Third Conditional correctly.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + past perfect (had listened), would have + past participle (would have understood)' — this is a perfect Third Conditional. It implies they did NOT listen and did NOT understand.
